title: Review of Health Service User population methodology | Stats NZ
content:
## Review of Health Service User population methodology
04 August 2022, 10:45am
10 October 2022 : We have included some additional text to clarify that the HSU contains information on gender rather than sex.
This report reviews the Ministry of Health (MoH) Health Service User population (HSU) and underlying methodology, considering its suitability as a denominator for measuring COVID-19 vaccine coverage and wider use.
Purpose Summary of key points Recommendations Background Strengths and limitations of HSU and ERP Analysis and findings from comparisons of HSU to PRP At risk populations and reference dates Ethnicity reporting Ethnicity prioritisation Dissemination Long-term developments Glossary Appendix 1: Terms of reference for Stats NZ review Appendix 2: Population derivations and weights
The Ministry of Health (MoH) has requested peer review from Stats NZ of the methods used to create the Health Service User population (HSU), considering its suitability as a denominator for measuring COVID-19 vaccine coverage, and wider use. The purpose of the review is to assess and document strengths, limitations (on use), suggested improvements, and/or development areas for the methodology used to create the HSU. MoH is keen to receive the findings as an independent check on the methods for building, use, and updates of the dataset.

## Summary of key points
- Stats NZ considers that the HSU has some advantages as a denominator for health-based analyses, including for the monitoring of COVID-19 vaccine coverage.
- The key advantage is the consistency of reporting of Māori and other ethnic groups in the numerator compared with the population denominator (for example, for vaccination rates). This use is consistent with Stats NZ’s advice that customers should validate the consistency of the numerator with the denominator when undertaking calculations by ethnicity.
- Another advantage of the HSU is the inclusion of visitors from overseas who may be using specific health services. This may be especially relevant if they are in New Zealand for several months, but not long enough to be statistically defined as a resident (that is, living in New Zealand).
- The HSU does have some limitations, notably for time series analyses because not everyone resident in New Zealand is in the HSU and its coverage varies over time.
- Use of the HSU does not preclude use of other population statistics (for example, Stats NZ’s estimated resident population – ERP) for health denominators. Indeed, the focus of Stats NZ’s estimates on the resident population means they are more suitable for some purposes, especially if the visitor (non-resident) use of the specific health service is inconsequential.
- Stats NZ has several recommendations to improve the derivation, reporting, and usefulness of the HSU. These are summarised in the following section.
- Comparisons between the 2021 HSU and Stats NZ population projections (to enable ethnic comparisons to be made), show closer alignment between the two measures of population compared with the 2020 equivalent, indicating that the addition of the vaccination data has improved the coverage of the HSU.
- Ethnicity differences between the two population measures, especially in Māori and Pacific populations, show the array of collection and sourcing challenges that exist with administrative data and highlights the need for careful consideration of what populations to use for what purposes. The lower representation of Māori in the HSU requires careful examination at source to identify whether it is driven by systemic issues, or genuine reporting choices by health service users.
- Comparison of the age distributions between the 2020 HSU and 2021 Stats NZ projections shows that decisions around the use of a static and outdated HSU contributed to inaccurate denominators in COVID-19 vaccination coverage estimates for older ages (for example, resulting in vaccination rates greater than 100 percent). Ageing the 2020 population into the correct ages for 2021 largely mitigates the discrepancies in older age groups. This was not as a result of methodological or conceptual errors in the HSU, but reflects decisions around choice of denominator.
- The use of prioritised ethnicity in the publications based on the 2020 HSU under-represents Pacific people by 15 percent compared with total response ethnicity estimates from the same 2020 HSU.
- The HSU should be derived ‘as at’ key reference dates (for example, 30 June, 31 December) before deriving a ‘mean’ population from these ‘as at’ estimates. Separating these steps simplifies the demographic accounting (updating the HSU), as well as providing alternative but consistent HSU estimates for different purposes.
- All aggregate HSUs should be derived from the HSU ‘as at’ unit record datasets – including revisions to those unit record datasets – so that there is one source of truth (internal consistency).
- Either the aggregate population distribution or unit record datasets can be used as the denominator for rates at a reference date, as these are consistent with each other.
- The aggregate population distribution, not unit record datasets, is the preferred denominator for rates over periods of time (for example, a year). These mean populations will average the reported attributes – age, ethnicity, and place of residence – of the population over the year. These aggregate mean populations over the year are consistent with (but not equal to) the ‘as at’ populations.
- All live births registered in New Zealand should be included in the HSU according to the date of birth of the child, regardless of their stated residence (that is, in New Zealand or overseas).
- All deceased whose deaths are registered in New Zealand should be included in the HSU for reference date(s) before the date of death, regardless of their stated residence (that is, in New Zealand or overseas).
- Vaccination data should be used to increase the HSU where people were not previously in the HSU at each reference date.
- Ethnicity data should be collected and reported according to ‘total response’ rather than ‘prioritised’, to better reflect the ethnicities reported by people using health services.
- Ethnicity data should be collected at the most detailed level of the ethnicity classification practicable, to give ultimate flexibility in reporting.
- The HSU should adhere to the principles of official statistics in its derivation and dissemination. This is because the HSU is an official statistic in its own right, but also used to derive other official statistics.
- The strengths and limitations of the HSU as a population measure and denominator should be openly published. This could be presented as done here in this report, either for the HSU alone, or in contrast with Stats NZ population estimates and projections.
- The rules by which people are added to, and removed from, the HSU, should be openly published. This could be presented as done here in this report. In the absence of explicit migrant arrivals and migrant departures data, the rules are important in defining the HSU. They also affect the population denominator for any derived rates, which should be clearly defined in dissemination (for example, of vaccination rates).
- MoH should implement some steps in their dissemination of the HSU to facilitate use, understanding, and transparency of the data and methods: a regular release and revision cycle for the HSU, as per published statistical principles advance notification of release dates including dates of revisions to the HSU publication of the HSU for selected disaggregations, with suitable rounding to protect the confidentiality of individuals explanation of how the HSU is derived for both technical and less technical audiences, including explanation of revisions or corrections to the HSU.
- a regular release and revision cycle for the HSU, as per published statistical principles
- advance notification of release dates including dates of revisions to the HSU
- publication of the HSU for selected disaggregations, with suitable rounding to protect the confidentiality of individuals
- explanation of how the HSU is derived for both technical and less technical audiences, including explanation of revisions or corrections to the HSU.
- How far back these revisions are applied requires an assessment by MoH of the value of such revisions against the downsides of revisions (small changes to derived series or inconsistencies), but MoH could consider a threshold of five years beyond which no revisions are made.
- The grouping of level 1 ethnic groups ‘European’ and ‘Other (including New Zealander)’ is appropriate when ‘Other’ responses are largely non-existent in numerator data sources. However, the level 1 ethnic group MELAA (Middle Eastern, Latin American, and African) should be separately reported where possible, as a relatively fast-growing and ethnically different grouping to European/Other.
Most of Stats NZ’s official population statistics relate to the resident population – the population usually living in a given geographic area. For many purposes, such as infrastructure planning in the health and education sectors, this is a very suitable population concept. The population using hospitals and schools is generally the population living in the surrounding area.
However, Stats NZ recognises that for different purposes, the resident population may not fully capture the population of interest. For example, in the transportation sector, the population using roads or public transport will include people who do not live in the immediate area (that is, visitors). Visitors – either from other parts of New Zealand or from overseas – can certainly place demands on local infrastructure and services. As some visitors can be in New Zealand for several months before they are statistically defined as a resident, such long-term visitors can also place demands on sectors such as housing and health that might normally be considered the preserve of residents.
Stats NZ actively encourages users of population statistics to consider the population of interest (see for example, Visitors to an area and the ‘service population' in Population statistics – user guide ). Official statistics of alternative populations (for example, de facto populations, peak populations) remain a development area, given the challenge of identifying the location of non-resident populations.
MoH’s HSU is an example of population statistics derived to measure a broader population than that of the resident population. COVID-19 vaccinations, for example, are available to all people in New Zealand, regardless of their place of residence.
The HSU is an estimate of the population using the health system in New Zealand in a given time period. A person is included in the HSU, for a given reference period, if they:
- use health services (including births and deaths) in the reference period, or
- are enrolled in a primary health organisation (PHO) during the reference period.
In deriving the HSU, births are considered ‘use of health services’ and included in the HSU if the birth was at a hospital, attended by a lead maternity carer, or registered with the Department of Internal Affairs (DIA). Births that occur at home, that are not attended by a lead maternity carer, and that are not registered, will not be included in the HSU, but will be included in updates of the HSU through subsequent use of health services (or birth registration). Stillbirths are excluded.
Entry into the population is therefore via births, PHO enrolments, and the use of other health services, and exit from the population is via deaths, lapsed PHO enrolments, and inactivity in health service use.
In practice, the HSU is a subset of the National Health Index (NHI) database. Most newborns are assigned an NHI number at birth and deceased persons are assigned an NHI number following their death, if they are not in the NHI database before their death.
The demographic variables age, gender, and ethnicity are derived from the NHI database as at the end of the reference period. Location/area is derived from the latest address notification before the end of the reference period.
The HSU is generally derived six months after the end of the reference period, reflecting the lag needed for the data used to construct the HSU to be sufficiently complete.
The above definition of the HSU refers to the ‘standard’ HSU. The mass rollout of COVID-19 vaccines in 2021 presented an opportunity for capturing, in the HSU, people who were not PHO-enrolled or active users of the health system. However, since the bulk of the COVID-19 vaccinations were received in the latter half of 2021 and the HSU is produced with a six-month lag, vaccine-only activity was not included in the previous HSU. In the interim, MoH constructed an experimental ‘hybrid’ HSU to study the impact of including COVID-19 vaccine-only activity in the HSU. This hybrid HSU is under review.
Two datasets were provided to Stats NZ for the purpose of this review – an instance of the ‘standard’ HSU (2020) and of the experimental ‘hybrid’ HSU (2020/2021). The 2020 HSU used as the denominator for estimating COVID-19 vaccine coverage is similar to the ‘standard’ HSU. The standard and hybrid versions differ in the length of the reference period and inclusion rules, mainly whether a person who is not PHO-enrolled is included in the HSU if their only interaction with the health system is a vaccination event. The HSU datasets include indicators to identify people whose only interaction with the health system was receiving vaccinations, or being PHO-enrolled, but not using any other health services. Table 1 summarises the rules used to construct the versions of the HSU.

### Demographic accounting
Stats NZ’s official estimates of the resident population (Estimated Resident Population or ERP) have a strongly defined concept, and additions to, and subtractions from, the population are clearly defined at all geographic levels using a standard population accounting equation:
P t Population at end of time period
P t-1 Population at start of time period (base population)
B            Births during time period
D            Deaths during time period
I             In-migration (arrivals) during time period
O           Out-migration (departures) during time period
These components of change may be estimated directly (for example, from death registrations) or indirectly (for example, by application of rates). Estimates by ethnicity often include an additional component for changes in ethnic identification (inter-ethnic mobility).
For population groups where frequent (for example, annual) migration and inter-ethnic mobility data are unavailable, direct estimation can be impractical. Population projections are an alternative type of estimation that can be used for both short-term and long-term purposes. Projections of the resident population involve assumptions about the future components of change – fertility (births), mortality (deaths), migration, and inter-ethnic mobility, either in terms of levels or rates. This is currently the approach used to indicate post-censal ethnic population changes at the subnational level.
The HSU should have a similar identifiable accounting equation. From our understanding, this will look like the population accounting equation with in- and out-migration defined as:
I              In-migration (arrivals) during time period viz additions (excluding births) to the HSU during the time period
O            Out-migration (departures) during time period viz subtractions (excluding deaths) from the HSU during the time period.
Strictly speaking, I – O is not net migration in a demographic sense, but the difference between people being added to the HSU through using health services or PHO enrolment, less people being removed from the HSU by an absence of health service use or lapsed PHO enrolment.

### Conceptual basis of ERP and HSU
The ERP and HSU have different target populations (figure 1). In principle, the ERP is an estimate of the population usually living (resident) in New Zealand at a point in time, and the HSU is a measure of the population in New Zealand using (or potentially using) the health system at a point in time. For both the ERP and HSU, mean populations over a period of time can be derived from the point in time estimates.
Figure 1 illustrates the different target populations of the ERP and HSU made up of four subpopulations:
A – People in the ERP and HSU (typically people resident in New Zealand who are using health services)
B – People in the ERP but not in the HSU (typically people resident in New Zealand who are not using health services)
C – People in the HSU but not in the ERP (typically people not resident in New Zealand who are using, or have used, health services – these may include visitors to New Zealand, and residents of New Zealand who have subsequently emigrated)
D – People not in the ERP or HSU (typically people not resident in New Zealand and not using health services because their visit to New Zealand is brief).
In practice, there may be some people resident in New Zealand who are excluded from the ERP and/or the HSU through under-coverage. There may also be a smaller number of people double-counted in the ERP and/or the HSU through over-coverage. This can occur despite the ERP including an explicit adjustment for census under-coverage and over-coverage, and the HSU having a rigorous de-duplication procedure.

## Strengths and limitations of HSU and ERP
Both the HSU and ERP have strengths and limitations that any user of them should be aware of. Depending on use, an advantage for one purpose may be a disadvantage for a different purpose.
- measure of the population using health services in New Zealand which relates better to the specific service population, irrespective of time spent in New Zealand
- ethnicity and place of residence identification likely to be more consistent across health data than with the independent census
- unit record data held by MoH provides an integrated dataset for use
- closer to real time updating reflecting changes in place of residence and ethnicity identification.
- conceptually complex population to define as potentially includes short-term visitors to New Zealand and people resident overseas, while excluding people resident in New Zealand who do not engage with health services
- requires signs-of-life filters to exclude people who have departed New Zealand, as migrant departures are not explicitly accounted for
- evidence of under-coverage with many vaccinated people not originally in HSU
- coverage will vary over time, with a peak in 2021–22 because of COVID-19 vaccination rollouts, so time series is inconsistent
- may include visitors to New Zealand – people who used health services during their short stay, but are not resident in New Zealand
- people enrolled with a PHO may be included despite emigrating and now resident overseas (potentially removed only after three years of non-contact)
- ethnicity and place of residence may be updated long after they change, depending on frequency of health service use
- use of private health services is incomplete.
- official resident population measure produced by Stats NZ
- well-established measure supported by extensive metadata
- updated regularly for different subpopulations, including revisions when new data becomes available
- consistent time series back to early 1996 (for all breakdowns)
- consistent with estimates and projections of the resident population which are regularly published or can be supplied (for example, customised DHB population projections by prioritised ethnicity, age, and sex, supplied annually to MoH)
- census elicits more multiple ethnicity responses than health data collections, resulting in higher ethnic populations in census-based ERP compared with HSU
- includes census coverage adjustments
- readily accessible from Stats NZ website and data tools ( Infoshare , NZ.Stat )
- aligns with international best practice on population and migration definitions and methods.
- measure of the ‘resident population’ so excludes visitors (people in New Zealand for less than 12 months), some of whom will be using health services
- ethnicity estimates are not updated as frequently as total population estimates, partly reflecting challenges in identifying ethnic migration (international and internal) and inter-ethnic mobility (changes in ethnic identification)
- ethnicity and place-of-residence identification in census-based ERP might be inconsistent with ethnic/place-of-residence identification in health-related numerator (for example, vaccination data)
- estimates are subject to revision as new data becomes available, including revisions after the next census.

### Uncertainties in the ERP, PRP, and HSU
The ERP at any reference date is a combination of the latest available census usually resident population counts, an adjustment for census coverage from a post-enumeration survey (PES), an adjustment for residents temporarily overseas on census night (out of scope for the census), and updates for demographic accounting (discussed earlier).
All of these data sources add uncertainty to the ERP. Estimates of the uncertainty – at least where it can be quantified – are detailed in Estimated resident population 2018: Data sources and methods . Table 2 (p14) of that report gives the uncertainty intervals for various demographic and geographic breakdowns.
In the analysis and comparisons of the HSU and resident populations (detailed in the next section), we use a projection of the resident population (PRP), largely to allow for comparisons across ethnicities, especially at a subnational level. The census-year ERP (at 30 June) has this full demographic and geographic breakdown, but not the ERP at subsequent reference dates. The assumptions made in deriving these projections, particularly assumptions around migration and inter-ethnic mobility, add uncertainty to the PRP to that existing in the census year ERP.
In addition to uncertainties in the ERP and PRP, the HSU also carries uncertainty, as is commonly the case with administrative data. The uncertainty in the HSU can stem from data collection and capture issues, such as non-standard collection of ethnicity which might discourage multiple response. Coverage issues, linkage errors, and duplication errors can also occur. Often such errors are difficult to quantify, particularly when dealing with large datasets like the HSU.
Therefore, caution must be used when drawing conclusions based on small differences in the population comparisons discussed in this report.

## Analysis and findings from comparisons of HSU to PRP
We compared the 2020 ‘standard’ HSU and 2021 ‘hybrid’ HSU datasets to the PRP to understand the differences and potential issues internal to the HSU. Projections of the resident population were used instead of estimates, because the projections provide annual subnational ethnicity breakdowns which are otherwise unavailable. In this section, we provide a high-level summary of findings which, together with the conceptual considerations, provides the basis for our recommendations.
The 2020 and 2021 HSU datasets include demographic variables like age, gender, ethnicity, and location (DHB). The ethnicity breakdown includes all six level-1 ethnic categories:
- Middle Eastern, Latin American, and African (MELAA)
- Other (including New Zealander).
Comparable DHB projections are available for the same demographic variables, except for ethnicity which is prioritised according to MoH requirements. MELAA, European, and ‘Other (including New Zealander)’ are grouped into an ‘Other’ category according to MoH requirements.
The projections contain information on sex, while the HSU contains information on gender. In the comparisons below, sex is compared with gender, and is referred to as sex.
All comparisons were done at an aggregate, and not unit record, level (for example, population counts by demographics). The description and derivation of the aggregate datasets is summarised in table 2.
Construction of aggregate datasets
2020 HSU – constructed from the 2020 standard HSU unit record file
2020 HSU – constructed from the 2020 standard HSU unit record file

Standard published criteria – the PRP is a measure of the resident population, and does not include short-term or long-term visitors (in New Zealand for less than 12 months), but does include residents who are temporarily overseas (for less than 12 months).

In addition to the comparisons of the HSU and PRP counts (by age, sex, prioritised ethnicity, and DHB), we analysed two possible limitations of the HSU derivation and its publication:
- Observable differences in populations when prioritised ethnicity is used instead of total response ethnicity. This highlights how certain subpopulations can be significantly under-represented by using prioritised ethnicity, even within the same HSU dataset.
- The effect of ageing birth cohorts from the 2020 HSU dataset to 2021, essentially ageing everyone by one year. This highlights how using an HSU for one year as a population denominator can affect rates when the numerator relates to a different year. For instance, when deriving the COVID-19 vaccine coverage rates, the 2020 HSU was used as the population denominator when a significant proportion of the vaccinations were given in the latter half of 2021.

### High-level findings of comparisons of HSU and PRP
In this section, we summarise comparisons between the HSU and the PRP for the two years 2020 and 2021. Two comparisons are used: the difference between the PRP and the HSU; and the percentage difference between the PRP and the HSU, relative to the HSU.

#### Comparisons by age-sex
For 2020, the differences in the youngest age groups (under 15 years), for both males and females, are relatively small, likely due to high rates of interaction with the health system through births and childhood immunisation (figure 2).
Among young adult age groups (15 to 29 years), the differences are relatively large for males compared with females. This likely reflects under-representation of young adult males in the HSU dataset, and the relatively higher interaction rates of females of childbearing age with the health system.
The differences for both males and females increase sharply in the oldest age groups (80+ years), with higher counts in the HSU. This largely reflects the smaller population numbers in those age groups resulting in larger percentage differences, rather than the HSU containing significantly larger numbers of older people.
Percentage difference in population by age-sex, PRP relative to HSU, 2020 and 2021
Percentage difference between PRP and HSU, relative to HSU, males by age, 2020 and 2021
Percentage difference between PRP and HSU, relative to HSU, females by age, 2020 and 2021
For 2021, using the hybrid HSU, the under-representation of the adult age groups is reduced, since the inclusion of the COVID-19 vaccination data of 2021 captures many more adults. This highlights the value of the hybrid HSU approach. It should be noted that in 2021, the HSU also captures populations that would not be considered part of the resident population (PRP) but received vaccinations due to the policy settings around vaccination access (that is, they were available to anyone in New Zealand, and not just the resident population).
The percentage differences at the oldest ages are again sensitive to small numbers in these age groups. However, it also reflects fundamental differences between the population alive at a specific date and the population ‘ever alive’ over a specified period (further discussed in At risk populations and reference dates ).

#### Comparisons by (prioritised) ethnicity
The 2020 and 2021 HSU datasets reported very different ethnic populations to the 2020 and 2021 PRP datasets (figure 3, table 3, and table 4). For both years, the HSU reports lower Māori and Asian ethnic populations, but more ‘Pacific peoples’ and ‘Other’ populations.
The higher Pacific population numbers in the HSU are too large to be explained solely by non-residents (for example, Recognised Seasonal Employment workers). Differences in reported ethnicities, the collection of those ethnicities, and coding of those ethnicities are all potentially contributing factors. For example, ‘Fijian Indian’ ethnicity being coded as ‘Pacific peoples’ with or without ‘Asian’, rather than solely in the ‘Asian’ category, potentially affects both Pacific and Asian population counts. To fully understand the differences, unit record level analysis within the IDI would be required, and that was not in scope for this review.
Difference in prioritised ethnicity, PRP relative to HSU, 2020 and 2021
Percentage difference between PRP and HSU, relative to HSU, by prioritised ethnic group, 2020 and 2021
Prioritised ethnic group
The differences in the ‘Pacific peoples’ and ‘Other’ ethnic populations increased from 2020 to 2021, while they decreased for the ‘Māori’ and ‘Asian’ populations between the two years. These changes are particularly pronounced for the ‘Asian’ and ‘Other’ categories. The increased differences in the ‘Pacific peoples’ and ‘Other’ categories going from 2020 to 2021 are likely due to the 2021 HSU capturing more people through the addition of vaccination data. However, both the PRP and HSU may not reflect the true ethnicity distributions because of inaccurate projection assumptions or collection and coding of ethnicity data.

#### Representation of Māori and Pacific populations
Delving into the Māori and Pacific populations, we observe similar patterns in the differences by single year of age as we did in the summaries in tables 3 and 4 above.
The Māori population is consistently smaller in the HSU compared with PRP, and the differences are largest among older children (ages 10 to 17 years) and young adults (ages 18 to 24 years) (figure 4). Reflecting the relatively younger age profile of Māori in general, and better capture of older age groups by the HSU, we see the differences declining among older Māori.
Compared with 2020, the 2021 hybrid HSU shows marginally smaller differences from the PRP; however, the general pattern of a lower population relative to the PRP remains.
Comparison of Māori ethnic population by age, PRP and HSU, 2020 and 2021
Māori ethnic population by age, HSU and PRP, 2020
Māori ethnic population by age, HSU and PRP, 2021
Māori ethnic population difference by age, PRP minus HSU, 2020
Difference by age (PRP - HSU), 2020
Māori ethnic population difference by age, PRP minus HSU, 2021
Difference (PRP - HSU)
In contrast to the Māori population, as noted previously, the Pacific population is higher in the HSU in both 2020 and 2021. This is observed across nearly all ages. With the inclusion of vaccination data in the 2021 HSU, this difference has grown, with the HSU even higher relative to the PRP. Most of the increase is among younger and middle-aged adults (20 to 45 years) (figure 5).
Comparison of prioritised Pacific ethnic population by age, PRP and HSU, 2020 and 2021

#### Comparisons by District Health Board
In this section, we compare population counts by District Health Board (DHB). At a total level, the PRP is higher than the 2020 HSU across most DHBs (figure 6). However, the 2020 HSU is higher than the PRP in Counties Manukau, Northland, Tairawhiti, Wairarapa, and Whanganui DHBs. An examination of linked unit record data may provide insight into the nature of these differences.
Comparison of DHB populations, PRP and HSU, 2020
Population in HSU and PRP, by District Health Board, 2020
District Health Board
Percentage difference between PRP and HSU, relative to HSU, by District Health Board, 2020
District Health Board
The pattern largely reverses in the 2021 comparison, with the 2021 HSU higher than the PRP across all DHBs (figure 7). The biggest turn-around with the addition of vaccination data is in Auckland – while the 2020 PRP for Auckland was about 3 percent higher than the 2020 HSU, the 2021 PRP is about 6 percent lower than the 2021 HSU. The increase in the HSU across the other DHBs, while smaller than the Auckland shift, is generally significant, indicating that the COVID-19 vaccination drive was necessary to capture people whose only interaction with the health system was through vaccination. As with ethnicity, the differences between PRP and HSU are a rationale for using the HSU to provide numerator-denominator consistency of reporting in place of residence.

### Using total response ethnicity compared with prioritised ethnicity
Prioritising ethnicity affects the apparent size of ethnic populations. In a ‘total response’ population, people are included in each of the ethnic groups that they identify with. The ethnic populations will sum to a value greater than the total population, because people can be in more than one ethnic population. In a prioritised ethnic population, people are included in only one ethnic group according to a specified priority order.
The impact can be illustrated using the 2020 HSU unit record data, where different ethnicity reporting affects the size of different ethnic populations and resulting ethnic population proportions (table 5 and figure 8). The Māori population is identical under both measures, as it retains the highest priority. However, the Pacific population decreases by 15 percent when prioritised ethnicity is used, a significant difference. Further, the European/Other population decreases by 12 percent, and its population proportion by 7 percentage points (from 67.6 percent to 60.5 percent), after prioritisation.

### Ageing the HSU from 2020 to 2021
Awareness of the HSU increased during its use in the calculation of vaccination rates during the COVID-19 pandemic response. Vaccinations of people who were not in the HSU (for example, non-residents) were used in the numerator for vaccination rates, but these people were not added to the published HSU, nor to the denominator used in deriving the rates. This understatement of the population denominator caused some inaccurate results (for example, vaccination rates that were greater than 100 percent).
A more subtle but important consequence of the static 2020 HSU was that by the time that most vaccinations were applied, the population had aged by one year. Comparing the 2020 HSU aged by one year with the 2021 PRP, especially in older age groups, reduces the differences between the two populations. The decision to maintain the static HSU caused inaccuracies in the population denominator, leading to understatement of the population by more than 10 percent at some older ages (figure 9). This issue will be effectively addressed by MoH’s plan to update the HSU annually from July 2022.
Age breakdown of populations in the 2021 PRP and 2020 HSU, showing the effect of an outdated HSU
Population in the 2020 HSU and 2021 PRP, by age (51+), showing the effect of an outdated HSU
Percentage difference between 2020 HSU and 2021 PRP, relative to 2020 HSU, by age (51+), showing the effect of an outdated HSU
Ageing the 2020 HSU appropriately gives much better alignment with the 2021 PRP, and the actual population that vaccinations were administered to, giving a more consistent pattern of differences in ages above 65 (figure 10). This highlights the importance of timely updates of the published HSU.
Age breakdown of populations in the 2021 PRP, and published 2020 HSU aged appropriately for 2021, showing much better alignment with the PRP
Population in the 2020 HSU and 2021 PRP, by age (51+), with the 2020 HSU population aged appropriately for 2021
Percentage difference between 2020 HSU and 2021 PRP, relative to 2020 HSU, by age (51+), with the 2020 HSU population aged appropriately for 2021
However, we also note that the vaccination denominator includes people who died in the second half of 2020 (that is, before vaccinations were available). While their inclusion was designed to estimate the population who would be eligible to receive the vaccine in 2021, the inclusion of those who have died inflates the population (especially among older people).
Removing all those who died in 2020 and ageing the 2020 HSU appropriately, produces a population much more consistent with the PRP (figure 11). This highlights the importance of carefully considering population concepts and applying fundamental demographic accounting when making decisions around population denominators. The methodology for establishing the HSU itself is not the cause of the misalignment, since the unit record data that constitutes the HSU allows for appropriate calculations. It is decisions around the choice of data and derivation of sub-populations from the HSU that affects the suitability of population denominators and resulting vaccination rates.
Age breakdown of populations in the 2021 PRP, and 2020 HSU aged appropriately for 2021 and removing deaths that occurred in 2020, showing even better alignment with the PRP
Population in the 2020 HSU and 2021 PRP, by age (51+), with the 2020 HSU population aged appropriately for 2021 and removing 2020 deaths
Percentage difference between 2020 HSU and 2021 PRP, relative to 2020 HSU, by age (51+), with the 2020 HSU population aged appropriately for 2021 and removing 2020 deaths

## At risk populations and reference dates
We recommend a change in the method of calculation of the HSU relative to key reference dates.
The current HSU attempts to represent an ‘ever alive’ population by including people alive at any time during, and events throughout, the calendar year. This means that people who died during the calendar year remain in the HSU. An ‘ever alive’ population over a period is typically unsuitable as a population denominator, as it inflates the ‘exposed-to-risk’ population over that period.
We recommend deriving the HSU for a reference period by averaging the HSUs derived ‘as at’ reference dates in and enclosing the reference period. For instance, calculating the HSU as at the start, middle, and end of the reference year, and deriving the population for the year as the weighted mean (average) of the populations at the ‘as at’ points (¼ population at start of the year + ½ population at middle of the year + ¼ population at end of the year).
The ‘as at’ populations could be calculated six-monthly, as at 30 June and 31 December, with mean populations calculated for year ended:
- 30 June Y as a weighted average of the populations as at 30 June Y – 1, 31 December Y – 1, and 30 June Y
- 31 December Y as a weighted average of the populations as at 31 December Y – 1, 30 June Y, and 31 December Y.
Alternatively, the HSU can be derived as the mean of the populations at the start and end of the reference year (each with a weight of ½). However, infrequent derivation of the ‘as at’ populations will increase the probability of people entering and leaving the population without appearing in any of the ‘as at’ populations.
A person will be included in the HSU as at a reference date, if:
- they are alive at the reference date, and
- they used health services (including vaccination) since the previous reference date, or
- they are enrolled with a PHO as at the reference date.
Strictly speaking, the calculation of events is at the end of the reference date (midnight or 24:00). For consistency, the HSU should be reported in terms of populations as at 30 June, not 1 July; and as at 31 December, not 1 January; etc.
Age should be calculated as the elapsed number of years from date of birth to the reference date. All attributes (age, sex, ethnicity, and location) are derived as at each reference date. The mean population for any combination of attributes is the average of the ‘as at’ populations for that combination of attributes.
There are several benefits of this recommended approach:
- Conceptually and methodologically, it is clearer to measure the population at a given date, and calculate mean populations from these ‘as at’ populations, rather than try to directly estimate a mean population. An ‘as at’ population: includes live births up to that date but not after excludes people who have died up to that date but not after includes entries up to that date but not after excludes exits up to that date but not after.
- includes live births up to that date but not after
- excludes people who have died up to that date but not after
- includes entries up to that date but not after
- excludes exits up to that date but not after.
The ‘as at’ population is therefore unambiguous as to who is included, and who is not. This avoids the counter-intuitive inclusion of people who have died, for example.
- This reinforces the importance of accurately defining the HSU: The HSU as at a given reference date is the population who has used health services during a given period of time and is alive at the given date. The mean HSU over a given period of time (for example, a year) is the average of the HSUs at the start, intermediate dates, and end of that period.
- The HSU as at a given reference date is the population who has used health services during a given period of time and is alive at the given date.
- The mean HSU over a given period of time (for example, a year) is the average of the HSUs at the start, intermediate dates, and end of that period.
- The availability of ‘as at’ and ‘mean year ended’ (‘mean YE’) HSUs meet different needs. An ‘as at’ population is a suitable denominator for stock populations, such as the population vaccinated at a given date (point prevalence). This population should exclude people who have died or emigrated. In contrast, a ‘mean YE’ is a suitable denominator for flow populations, such as the population that received a vaccination over a year (incidence). For a population that changes significantly over the year, the mean population minimises the bias from using the population at the start or end of the year.
- An ‘as at’ population is a suitable denominator for stock populations, such as the population vaccinated at a given date (point prevalence). This population should exclude people who have died or emigrated.
- In contrast, a ‘mean YE’ is a suitable denominator for flow populations, such as the population that received a vaccination over a year (incidence). For a population that changes significantly over the year, the mean population minimises the bias from using the population at the start or end of the year.
For someone who dies in New Zealand, has their death registered in New Zealand, and is not otherwise included in the HSU, they should be included in the HSU at the latest reference date after their date of birth but before the date of death, regardless of their stated residence (that is, in New Zealand or overseas). There will be few people who satisfy this criterion; mainly visitors to New Zealand who die in New Zealand, and residents not enrolled with PHOs and have not used the health system. For example, someone who dies in July should appear in the HSU at 30 June.
For the mean population over a year, this population will include people who were part of the HSU at one or more of the ‘as at’ dates. For example, people alive at the start and end of the year will be included. People alive at the start of the year who died during the year will be included, but with a weighting of ½ (if the mean is the average of the start and end populations). Similarly, people who entered the population through birth or migration during the year will be included, but with a weighting of ½.
Note that some people who enter and depart the population between the ‘as at’ reference dates will be excluded from the ‘as at’ populations and therefore from the mean HSU. Babies who are born and die between the ‘as at’ reference dates will be included in births and deaths data but excluded from the HSU. Similarly, people who ‘immigrated’ then ‘emigrated’ (in the context of the HSU) between the ‘as at’ reference dates will be excluded from the HSU, even if they used health services during their short stay. As most of these short stay visitors will not be using health services (including receiving vaccinations), their omission is likely to be insignificant.
If these visitors are important – such as Recognised Seasonal Employment workers who may be in New Zealand for several months but not at the key 30 June and 31 December reference dates – then there is an option to derive the ‘as at’ populations more frequently, say quarterly. The mean population is then calculated as ⅛ population at start of the year + ¼ population at end of first quarter + ¼ population at middle of the year + ¼ population at end of third quarter + ⅛ population at end of the year.
Clearly, it is important to identify and differentiate the ‘as at’ and ‘mean YE’ HSUs in any dissemination.
Population derivation and weights are further discussed in Appendix 2: Population derivations and weights .
Reporting of ethnicity is highly dependent on the context and environment of collection, including the timing and method of collection, as well as the perceived use of the information collected. Stats NZ’s advice is that customers should validate the consistency of the numerator with the denominator when undertaking calculations by ethnicity. The HSU therefore has an important advantage in that the reporting of Māori and other ethnic groups is inherently consistent between the numerator and population denominator, even if this reporting is inconsistent with reporting elsewhere (for example, in the Census of Population and Dwellings).

## Ethnicity prioritisation
People can and do identify with multiple ethnicities. Official statistics published by Stats NZ have used ‘total response’ for the basis of most ethnicity statistics since the mid-1990s. This is where people are included in each ethnic group that they identify with. This approach is consistent with the findings from the 2004 Review of the Measurement of Ethnicity and the subsequent Ethnicity New Zealand Standard Classification 2005 .
When using ‘total response’, ethnic group populations overlap and cannot be summed to give the total population. However, this is generally inconsequential when the objective is to fairly indicate and represent the population identifying with a given ethnicity.
Prioritised ethnicity data are not recommended. Prioritisation re-classifies a person with multiple ethnicity responses to just one ethnicity or ethnic group. There are several problems with choosing one ethnicity for a person who has said that they belong to more than one ethnicity:
- Standard ethnicity questions ask a person to choose which ethnic group or groups they belong to. The question usually accepts more than one answer and prioritisation goes against the principle of self-choice.
- People are not asked to specify one main ethnicity or ethnic group and do not know that prioritisation would arbitrarily make this choice for them.
- Prioritisation misrepresents the number of people who identify with an ethnic group.
Multiple ethnicity is most prevalent among younger ages, reflecting children born to parents of different ethnicities. Ethnic prioritisation will increasingly misrepresent ethnic populations given the following trends:
- above average growth of minority ethnic populations such as the broad Māori, Asian, Pacific and MELAA (Middle Eastern, Latin American, and African)
- increasing rates of multiple ethnicity.
The Ministry of Health has Ethnicity Data Protocols which are standards set by the Health Information Standards Organisation (HISO) published for the New Zealand health and disability sector. These protocols provide good guidance on the collection, processing, and output of ethnicity data in the sector.
Stats NZ recommends use of ‘total response’ rather than ‘prioritised’ ethnicity in all statistics. The current review of the ethnicity standard is scheduled to finish in June 2023. This could see total response ethnicity become a mandated data standard by the Government Chief Data Steward later in 2023.

### Ethnicity disaggregation
Ethnicity data should always be collected at the lowest level of the classification as practicable, which gives the ultimate flexibility on dissemination.
For practical reasons (for example, population size and robustness), it is often necessary to aggregate ethnic population statistics to the highest level of the ethnicity classification on dissemination. However, we encourage disaggregation to lower levels (2, 3, 4, or 5) where possible.
Given the current and projected growth of the broad MELAA population (for example, Stats NZ national ethnic population projections ), separate publication of this grouping is recommended where practicable, rather than grouping with the much larger European/Other (including New Zealander) population.
The production and dissemination of official statistics are guided by some fundamental principles, such as:
- United Nations Fundamental Principles of Official Statistics
- OECD Good Statistical Practice Toolkit
- Stats NZ Release Practices Policy
The HSU and vaccination statistics produced by the MoH can both be regarded as official statistics. Official statistics are all statistics produced by government departments. Official statistics are defined in section 2 of the Statistics Act:
They are statistics derived by government departments from:
a. statistical surveys
b. administrative and registration records and other forms and papers the statistical analyses of which are published regularly, or are planned to be published regularly, or could reasonably be published regularly
To strengthen use, understanding and transparency of the HSU methodology and data we recommend the following:
- A regular release and revision cycle for the HSU. ‘As at’ populations should be calculated at least six-monthly, which would enable both June and December mean year-ended estimates to be derived. Populations should be revised at least six-monthly to incorporate updated data (for example, birth and death registrations). How far back these revisions are applied requires an assessment by MoH of the value of such revisions against the downsides of revisions (small changes to derived series or inconsistencies), but consider a threshold of five years (beyond which no revisions are made). Revisions should follow the principles of Stats NZ’s Policy for revising previously published statistics . An example of the policy in practice is Applying the Stats NZ revisions policy to outputs from the Household Economic Survey .
- ‘As at’ populations should be calculated at least six-monthly, which would enable both June and December mean year-ended estimates to be derived.
- Populations should be revised at least six-monthly to incorporate updated data (for example, birth and death registrations). How far back these revisions are applied requires an assessment by MoH of the value of such revisions against the downsides of revisions (small changes to derived series or inconsistencies), but consider a threshold of five years (beyond which no revisions are made).
- Revisions should follow the principles of Stats NZ’s Policy for revising previously published statistics . An example of the policy in practice is Applying the Stats NZ revisions policy to outputs from the Household Economic Survey .
- Advance notification of release dates including dates of revisions to the HSU, similar to the Stats NZ release calendar .
- Publication of the HSU for selected disaggregations, with suitable rounding to protect the confidentiality of people.
- Explanation of how the HSU is derived for both technical and less technical audiences, including explanation of revisions or corrections to the HSU.
These dissemination recommendations recognise that the HSU is a valuable data resource, but users of it must be able to easily access fundamental information about its derivation and limitations.
For example, there is useful information about the HSU on the ‘HSU Population’ tab of the ‘COVID-19 vaccination data’ spreadsheet near the bottom of the COVID-19: Vaccine data webpage. This information would benefit from publication in a more prominent and searchable format.

## Long-term developments
There are long-term options beyond 2022 for both Stats NZ and MoH to develop their population datasets further.
Stats NZ, for example, is exploring census transformation options that make greater use of administrative data and potentially allow alternative population measures to be produced with higher frequency and granularity. This would include estimates of the resident population consistent with the existing ERP, but might also include alternative estimates which include long-term visitors, for example. The HSU can, in conjunction with linked administrative data, potentially be used to derive alternative population estimates.
The HSU target population is the population using, or potentially using, health services, and as such should include people using health services and people usually living in New Zealand but not using health services (that is, HSU under-coverage). Conceptually, this target population is the union of the HSU and ERP populations (described in figure 1). If migration data is used to identify visitors, then non-visitors using the health system can be upweighted to adjust for the under-coverage in the HSU.
The MoH might also want to explore options which better measure the population of interest:
- integration of international travel and migration data (border movements) which allows people to be included/excluded rather than relying on recorded health use
- increase the frequency of derivation from six-monthly ‘as at’ reference dates to quarterly, monthly, or daily (‘real time’)
- identification of multiple ethnicities to improve ‘total response’ ethnicity data
- identification of iwi to better support Te Tiriti o Waitangi partners.
de facto population – statistical basis for a population in terms of those present in a given area. The ‘census night population count’ and the discontinued ‘estimated de facto population’ are both statistical measures of the de facto population.
estimated resident population (ERP) – estimate of the population that usually lives in New Zealand, or an area of New Zealand. The estimates are higher than the census usually resident population count because they include an allowance for net census undercount and residents who are temporarily overseas at the time of the census.
health service user population (HSU) – estimate of the population that uses health services in New Zealand. This may include people living overseas as well as those living in New Zealand. The HSU also includes people who have not necessarily used health services:
- people born in New Zealand
- people enrolled with primary health organisations
- people who die in New Zealand.
resident population – statistical basis for a population in terms of those who usually live in a given area. The ‘census usually resident population count’ and the ‘estimated resident population’ (ERP) are both statistical measures of the resident population.
Projections of the resident population (PRP) are also produced, typically using the latest ERP as a base (starting point) and applying assumptions about the future components of population change (for example, birth rates, death rates, and migration).
service population – statistical basis for a population in terms of those who use, or potentially use, a given service in a given area.

## Appendix 1: Terms of reference for Stats NZ review
- The review is based on the methodology of the planned interim 2021 HSU.
- MoH has well-documented information on the methods they used to develop the HSU dataset, along with the concepts the HSU are looking to measure.
- Stats NZ will only assess what is provided to Stats NZ in a written form, or as data.
- Stats NZ will have access to people at MoH to liaise with as needed for this review.
- Stats NZ will not be charging MoH for this work.
- Stats NZ are able to get preliminary interim 2021 HSU data: by age by sex by SA2 and DHB by age by sex by ethnicity.
- by age by sex by SA2 and DHB
- by age by sex by ethnicity.
The scope of the review will focus on a conceptual and methodological review of the new interim 2021 HSU methodology. This will include looking at the strengths, weaknesses, and possible improvements/development areas and/or limitations of the concepts and methods. Comparison of the data with official Stats NZ population measures will be included in the review, but will be a minor part, and for the purposes of feeding into the examination of the methods and possible improvements/limitations.
- Theoretical/conceptual examination of the method, data sources, and applied measurement concepts (ie, are the concepts sound?)
- Conceptual exploration of possible improvements and limitations, including an articulation of the conceptual difference from official Stats NZ population measures
- Comparison of: Population across demographics and geographies Ethnicities at a national level Comparisons of the HSU may be done with the Stats NZ estimated and projected resident populations
- Population across demographics and geographies
- Ethnicities at a national level
- Comparisons of the HSU may be done with the Stats NZ estimated and projected resident populations
- Brief review of the release processes for this data and consideration and application of best practice in publication, release, and update of data
- Summary of findings from the above, in a format that is presentable to decisions makers at both Stats NZ and MoH.
- Technical review of code, or assessing the alignment of the concepts with the computations conducted (ie, have the concepts above been correctly implemented?)
- No assurances around methodological or implementation accuracy
- No unit record level analysis of the data, or use of the Integrated Data Infrastructure (IDI) for assessment of this dataset (ie, Stats NZ will not examine and provide comment on different uses of ethnicity, possibilities of double counting people, linkage, and/or duplication issues, and coverage assessments beyond the theoretical/conceptual examination of the method as a whole).

### Resourcing, roles, and responsibilities
The Stats NZ team will be a mix of analysts from the Population Insights and Methods and Design units.
Methods and Design will focus on the methodological parts, including the examination of the method and possible methodological improvements. Population Insights will focus on the conceptual parts, including the contextual implications and theoretical comparison of the different methods.

## Appendix 2: Population derivations and weights

### Unit record populations
The recommended approach applies to HSU unit record analyses, as well as aggregate HSU statistics.
The unit record dataset ‘as at’ the key reference dates (for example, 30 June and/or 31 December) is reasonably intuitive. They are the unit records of the population alive at those dates. The aggregate ‘as at’ populations are derived by aggregating the corresponding unit-record ‘as at’ populations.
A unit-record mean dataset over a year is more complex than an aggregate mean dataset. It involves merging the available ‘as at’ unit record datasets and weighting each record according to the frequency that each record appears in the ‘as at’ datasets.
Alternatively, if in future the HSU is calculated on a daily basis, or incorporates migration data, each record can be weighted according to the ‘person years’ the person contributes to the HSU over the reference period. For instance, if a person is in the HSU for 125 days out of the year, they are assigned a weight of 125/365 = 0.34 years.
The records in a unit-record mean dataset represent the population alive at any time during the year (strictly speaking, alive at the ‘as at’ reference dates). This will be higher than the aggregate ‘mean YE’ population calculated as a weighted average of the ‘as at’ populations unless those weights are also applied to the unit records.
To achieve the latter, unit records in the merged dataset should carry a weight relative to the number of input ‘as at’ datasets. For example, if the ‘as at’ datasets are six-monthly:
- a unit record that appears in all 3 ‘as at’ datasets (start of year, mid-year and end of year), will carry a weight of 1
- a unit record that appears at the start of the year, but not in the other ‘as at’ datasets, will carry a weight of ¼
- a unit record that appears at the start of year and mid-year, but not at the end of the year, will carry a weight of ¾
If the ‘as at’ datasets are quarterly:
- a unit record that appears in all 5 ‘as at’ datasets (start of Q1, Q2, Q3 and Q4; and end of Q4), will carry a weight of 1
- a unit record that appears at the start of Q1, but not in the other ‘as at’ datasets, will carry a weight of ⅛
- a unit record that appears at the start and end of Q1, but not in the other ‘as at’ datasets, will carry a weight of ⅜
For rates at a date, either the aggregate population distribution or unit-record population dataset ‘as at’ that date can be used as the denominator, as the former is derived directly from the latter.
For rates over a period, the aggregate mean population distribution, not unit-record mean datasets, is the preferred denominator. The mean populations will average the reported attributes – age, ethnicity, and place of residence – of the population over the year. These aggregate mean populations over the year are consistent with (but not equal to) the ‘as at’ populations.
Where unit-record mean datasets are needed (for example, for integration with other health datasets), the population weights should be carried through. For attributes that have changed over the year – notably ethnicity and place of residence – these can be reported as at the end of the period on the basis that they are most accurate at that reference date. However, these unit record datasets will not be consistent with the ‘as at’ populations because those attributes can be weighted at an aggregate level but make less sense to do so for unit record analyses.

In this example, we derive a unit-record mean population from ‘as at’ datasets of individuals with recorded age, sex and area attributes. There are several options to derive a unit-record mean population from the ‘as at’ populations. Arguably the most accurate is a mean population derived in terms of the contributions of individuals to the subpopulations of interest measured in person-years spent in those subpopulations. In table 9, we have derived this unit-record mean population, for year ended 31 Dec 2022, from the ‘as at’ populations in table 8. Using this approach requires consideration of the values of the attributes. One option is to set categorical attributes to their values at the end of the reference period and numeric attributes to their weighted average over the ‘as at’ dates. Alternatively, categorical attributes can be set to their values at the last ‘as at’ date in which the individual appeared in the reference year. If the approach described in table 9 is not suitable, then we recommend that aggregate mean populations be used as the denominator for rates derived over a reference period, since changes in attributes over the reference period are naturally accounted for.
The weighting is based on the frequency at which the ‘as at’ populations are derived and the number of ‘as at’ populations contributing to the mean YE population.
For the unit-record populations, a person appearing in only one ‘as at’ population, say at time point t, enters the population sometime after the previous ‘as at’ point, t – 1, and exits the population sometime before the next ‘as at’ point, t + 1. A common approach is to assume that the person entered and exited exactly at the midpoints between the ‘as at’ dates. The person years (or weight) the person contributes to the reference year then depends on whether the reference year spans only the first, only the second, or both midpoints.
An example for ‘as at’ populations derived six-monthly and year-ended 31 December reference years is depicted in figure 12. For reference year 2021, a person appearing in the population as at 31 December 2020 only, or as at 31 December 2021 only, is assigned a weight of ¼, while a person appearing in the population as at 30 June 2021 only is assigned a weight of ¼ + ¼ = ½. A person in the population at all three time points, is assigned the sum of these weights, totalling 1.
The aggregate mean YE population is an approximation to the person-years population, and therefore the weighting of the ‘as at’ aggregate populations is analogous to the individual weighting. An ‘as at’ aggregate population P t represents a population that is constant between the midpoints of the intervals to the left and right of it. For instance, in figure 12 where weights are derived six-monthly, the aggregate population as at 30 June 2021 represents the population over the period 31 March 2021 to 30 September 2021.
